draft.introduction: The Manichaean Psalm Book is a collection of hymns and psalms used in Manichaean worship, preserved in Coptic in the same Medinet Madi discovery that yielded the Kephalaia. The psalms are attributed to various early Manichaean teachers and are organized into cycles: Psalms of Thomas, Psalms of Heraclides, Psalms of the Bema, and others. They represent the devotional and liturgical heart of Manichaean religious life. The psalms are among the most beautiful religious poetry of late antiquity. They express the longing of the trapped light-soul for liberation, the grief of the divine particles imprisoned in matter, and the ecstatic joy of reunion with the Light. The recurring theme is identity: “I am in everything, I am in the light”—the realization that the divine is not separate from the self but is the deepest truth of what the self actually is. This is Manichaean theosis: not becoming divine, but recognizing that one has always been divine, a fragment of the original Light temporarily lost in Darkness.

translation.original_ref: Coptic Manichaean MS, Medinet Madi, Psalm Book p. 203
translation.text: I am in everything. I bear the heavens, I am the foundation. I support the earths, I am the light that shines forth, that gives joy to the souls. I am the life of the world: I am the milk that is in all trees. I am the sweet water that is under the children of matter.
translation.original_text: ⲁⲣⲕⲕ ⲑⲣ ⲣⲉⲧⲓⲃⲉ ⲣⲓⲙ ⲁⲓⲥⲓ ⲣⲓⲙⲉⲥⲉ ⲣⲣⲑⲥⲉ

translation.original_ref: Coptic Manichaean MS, Psalm Book p. 204
translation.text: I bore these things until I had fulfilled the will of my Father. The First Man is my father. I have borne all these burdens. I came forth from his struggle. The five powers are my mother. They brought me into the world.

translation.original_ref: Coptic Manichaean MS, Psalm Book p. 220
translation.text: Come to me, my kinsmen, my light-givers, my limbs of light which I put on when I went to the contest. Awake, O soul, from the drunkenness in which you have been, and the sleep. Follow me to the place of the exalted land where you dwelt in the beginning.

translation.original_ref: Coptic Manichaean MS, Psalm Book p. 187
translation.text: A soul cried out from the midst of the darkness. It said: “Where am I? Who has brought me to this place? I was a king’s daughter, I sat in my father’s kingdom. He sent me down to the earth, he gave me a garment of flesh. I have forgotten my royalty, I serve in the house of my captors.”

translation.original_ref: Coptic Manichaean MS, Psalm Book p. 189
translation.text: “But my father sent his son to me. He came down to me in my lowly state. He said to me: ‘Rise up, O soul, you are from the race of kings. Look at the garment of light that awaits you. Remember the kingdom from which you have been sent. Your crown of glory is kept for you.’”

translation.original_ref: Coptic Manichaean MS, Psalm Book p. 175
translation.text: We are particles of light. We fell into the darkness, we were swallowed by death. Now we cry to the living ones, the sons of God: “Save us from the darkness, draw us up to the light, gather us into the garners of life. For we are your own members, scattered in the world.”

translator_notes.note: The declaration “I am in everything” is spoken not by an individual but by the Living Self—the collective divine light scattered throughout creation. This is a pantheistic statement within a dualist framework: the divine is in all things not because all things are divine, but because particles of the original Light are trapped in everything material. The Manichaean project is to extract these particles and return them to their source.

The motif of the soul as a king’s daughter who has forgotten her royalty is strikingly parallel to the “Hymn of the Pearl” in the Acts of Thomas, one of the most famous Gnostic poems. Both texts present the same narrative arc: divine origin, descent into matter, forgetfulness, and awakening through a messenger from the Father. Whether the Manichaean version depends on the earlier Gnostic text or both draw on a common source is debated.

The phrase “we are your own members, scattered in the world” reflects the Manichaean doctrine that the cosmos is literally the body of the First Man, broken apart and distributed through matter. Salvation is therefore the reassembly of a divine body—a corporate theosis in which individual liberation contributes to the healing of a cosmic wound.
